coredata.dc:title: Microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon quantum dots and their integration with TiO2 nanotubes for enhanced photocatalytic degradation
coredata.dc:creator: Rawat, Jyoti
Sharma, Himani
Dwivedi, Charu
coredata.dc:description: This study demonstrates the successful synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) using the microwave method. Additionally, the hydrothermal method is employed to synthesize CQDs-decorated TiO2 nanotubes (CQDs@TNT) from CQDs decorated TiO2 (CQDs@TiO2). The CQDs induce the quantum confinement effect, observed by employing fluorescence spectroscopy (FL). The decoration of CQDs also results in a 2.92 eV reduction in the band gap in CQDs@TNT. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) reveals the elemental composition of both CQDs and TiO2, including TiO, CO, Ti-O-C, CC, and CO bonds. XRD spectroscopy confirms a reduction in crystallite size and negative microstrain due to compressive strength. The degradation study demonstrates the remarkable photocatalytic activity and stability of CQDs@TNT in the presence of tetracycline (TC) as the target pollutant under simulated visible light. The time dependence of the photodegradation of TC is also studied, and the system is found to be following pseudo second-order kinetics.

The authors acknowledge Mr. K.K. Singh, Radiation and Photochemistry Division, BARC Mumbai, for collecting TGA data.

head.ce:title: Microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon quantum dots and their integration with TiO2 nanotubes for enhanced photocatalytic degradation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Physical Sciences, Doon University, Dehradun, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Physical Sciences, Doon University, Dehradun, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, School of Physical Sciences, Doon University, Dehradun, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics, School of Physical Sciences, Doon University, Dehradun, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study demonstrates the successful synthesis of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) using the microwave method. Additionally, the hydrothermal method is employed to synthesize CQDs-decorated TiO2 nanotubes (CQDs@TNT) from CQDs decorated TiO2 (CQDs@TiO2). The CQDs induce the quantum confinement effect, observed by employing fluorescence spectroscopy (FL). The decoration of CQDs also results in a 2.92 eV reduction in the band gap in CQDs@TNT. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) reveals the elemental composition of both CQDs and TiO2, including TiO, CO, Ti-O-C, CC, and CO bonds. XRD spectroscopy confirms a reduction in crystallite size and negative microstrain due to compressive strength. The degradation study demonstrates the remarkable photocatalytic activity and stability of CQDs@TNT in the presence of tetracycline (TC) as the target pollutant under simulated visible light. The time dependence of the photodegradation of TC is also studied, and the system is found to be following pseudo second-order kinetics.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Quantum dots (QDs) are colloidal nanocrystalline semiconductors and have received significant attention due to their unique light-emitting properties, positioning them as new luminescent probes [1]. These QDs are typically spherical and exhibit diameters ranging from 1 to 12 nm, containing a discrete cluster of comparatively few atoms within each dot. Noteworthy characteristics of QDs include size-dependent photoluminescence, excellent photostability, prolonged emission lifetimes, and excitation-independent photoluminescence [2]. QDs exhibit remarkable resistance to photobleaching, making them a promising alternative to traditional fluorescent dyes [3]. Furthermore, their diminutive size facilitates robust quantum confinement of photo-excited charges within a confined space referred to as a quantum box, thereby shortening the path length for the transport of photo-excited charges during photocatalytic reactions [4].

CQDs represent a category of nanoscale spherical carbon fluorescent materials that possess distinctive benefits such as light stability and sensitivity. Various biomass sources, including lycium and Citrus limon peels, have been employed in the synthesis of CQDs [5]. Carbon, a conventionally non-toxic element, has recently become a central point in research due to the emergence of CQDs. These CQDs have garnered significant interest owing to their favorable attributes, including high aqueous solubility, environmental friendliness, low toxicity, as well as distinctive upconverted photoluminescence (PL) behavior, and the ability to facilitate photo-induced electron transfer [6,7]. CQDs could play a significant role in the advancement of semiconductor photoelectric materials. CQDs are typically characterized as monodisperse quasi-spherical nanoparticles with a core structure resembling multilayered graphene, accompanied by a substantial presence of surface bound oxygen and nitrogen-containing functional groups [8]. Based on their internal and surface structures, CQDs are broadly categorized into three groups: carbon nanodots (CNDs, featuring multiple embedded graphene layers within a sp3 structure), graphene quantum dots (GQDs, comprising one to a few graphene layers with relatively broader lateral dimensions), and polymer dots (PDs, primarily composed of a polymeric structure devoid of a crystalline core) [9]. These categories possess distinct characteristics, and it is worth noting that the physical and chemical properties of CQDs are greatly influenced by the choice of precursors and the conditions employed during their synthesis [10]. Various adjustments applied to the precursors during CQDs synthesis, as well as post-synthesis modifications, can be used to tailor and confer specific properties upon CQDs for diverse applications such as photocatalysis, drug delivery, anti-pathogen, and biosensing [11].

CQD decorated photocatalysts offer various benefits, including expanded light absorption into the near-infrared spectrum facilitated by the up-conversion photoluminescence characteristics of CQDs. Additionally, the robust electron transport properties contribute to the enhanced separation of electron-hole pairs, while the stability is improved through the coupling of CQDs in composites, such as the core-shell structure. These distinctive and outstanding features make composites based on CQDs promising candidates for photocatalysts in applications such as energy conversion or environmental purification [12]. A significant interest has been placed on CQDs in biosensing in particular because of their distinctive electrochemical and fluorescent characteristics, positioning them as a viable replacement to conventional organic fluorescent dyes and semiconductor QDs, with the added benefit of reduced biohazard concerns [10]. On the other hand, a paramount challenge in managing water pollution is the removal of hazardous organic compounds from wastewater. Numerous techniques have been employed to reduce contaminants, including burial, incineration, reclamation, etc. [11]. In a short period of time, photodegradation has garnered substantial attention due to its cost-effectiveness and minimal environmental impact. Various semiconductor materials, including SnO2, ZnO, Fe2O3, TiO2, and C3N4, have been explored as photocatalysts [13–15]. Among these, TiO2 has emerged as the most promising for both practical and fundamental research, owing to its exceptional properties like non-toxicity, chemical stability, affordability, safety, and high degradation efficiency [16]. Numerous works have been directed towards crafting diverse morphologies of TiO2, encompassing nanotubes, hierarchical microspheres, porous networks, nanorods, and hollow spheres, in an effort to enhance photocatalytic performance [15].

In contrast to conventional nanostructures, TiO2 nanotubes are distinguished as highly efficient photocatalysts, primarily attributable to their unique one-dimensional (1D) nanostructure. These porous nanotubes exhibit a double-walled configuration that endows them with a substantial specific surface area and porosity [17]. This distinctive feature enhances their capacity for efficient photon utilization, solution convection, and dye adsorption, consequently conferring exceptional photocatalytic capabilities [17]. TiO2 nanotube photocatalysis has emerged as a potential approach for water treatment, specifically for the mineralization and elimination of organic pollutants. While photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants can yield end-products, these by-products are often unclear. Additionally, the intermediates generated during the process may have adverse effects on the environmental ecosystem, highlighting the need for an assessment of the solution's toxicity variations following photocatalytic reactions [18]. TC, a commonly used antibiotic in both medicine and animal husbandry, has been found in various water bodies because of its widespread misuse, posing major environmental concerns [19].

The presence of TC in the environment has the potential to stimulate the development of resistant bacteria and genes, leading to significant impacts on ecosystem functionality [20]. To address this issue, semiconductor photocatalysts play a key role in environmentally conscious photocatalytic technology that harnesses solar energy as part of its removal of TC. While intermediates resulting from TC photocatalytic degradation and ozonation have been found, it is noteworthy that the intermediates and potential mechanism of TC photocatalytic degradation have received limited attention in existing research efforts [21].

This study introduces a straightforward approach for the synthesis of a new metal-free photocatalyst known as CQDs decorated TiO2 nanotube (CQDs@TNT). This novel composite aims to mitigate the recombination of photoinduced charges, thereby significantly enhancing photocatalytic performance in TC degradation while maintaining excellent stability. We conducted a comprehensive characterization of the chemical composition, phase, and morphology of the CQDs@TNT material. Additionally, we propose a comprehensive mechanism for the photodegradation process facilitated by the CQDs@TNT composite. This developed method offers a potential alternative for the development of photocatalysts dedicated to the degradation of organic dyes, contributing to the conservation of environment.

The synthesis of CQDs was carried out via microwave synthesis. Briefly, a solution comprising allylamine and ascorbic acid was prepared in water. Subsequently, the mixture underwent a 2 min sonication process and was then subjected to microwave irradiation at 250 W using a standard microwave oven for a duration of 5 min. Following ordinary cooling to room temperature, the resulting powders were subjected to multiple rounds of purification through precipitation with water, followed by separation via centrifugation. The purified CQDs were subsequently dispersed in water, resulting in the formation of a yellow-colored CQDs solution (Step 1, Fig. 1 ). It is worth noting that all the initial materials employed in the CQDs synthesis process are safe for the environment and non-toxic.

To synthesize TiO2 nanoparticles (NPs), we initiated the process by combining titanium tetrachloride (TiCl4) and absolute ethanol (CH3CH2OH) in a 1:5 ratio, with TiCl4 being added dropwise into the ethanol (Supplementary information). TiO2 NPs in the anatase phase were produced after the resulting TiO2 powder was calcined for a duration of 2 h in the furnace operating at 500 °C within an ambient environment [22]. To create CQDs decorated TiO2 (CQDs@TiO2), the initially synthesized CQDs were blended with TiO2 NPs and stirred for a duration of 5 min. The prepared mixture was subsequently subjected to drying at 80 °C (Step 2, Fig. 1). For the synthesis of CQDs decorated TiO2 nanotubes (CQDs@TNT), we initiated the process by mixing 0.1 g of CQDs@TiO2 with a (10 mol/L) 60 mL NaOH aqueous solution. This mixture underwent ultrasonic treatment for 15 min, after which it was transferred into an autoclave and exposed to hydrothermal treatment for a duration of 24 h at 200 °C. Following this step, thorough washing with an HCl solution continued until achieving a neutral pH. The obtained yield was then subjected to calcination for a period of 4 h at 550 °C. These successive stages culminated in the production of CQDs@TNT (Step 2, Fig. 1).

The composition and structure of the synthesized material were assessed through XPS and XRD techniques. TGA was performed on the powder samples utilizing a thermal analyzer system. The band gap and valance band shift among TiO2, CQDs@TiO2, and CQDs@TNT was also investigated using UV-DRS measurements and UPS technique. HR-TEM analysis was performed using a Tecnai G2 20 (FEI) S-Twin transmission electron microscope, operating at 300 kV. To comprehensively assess the spectral properties, kinetic study, effect of pH, and adsorption study of the synthesized materials UV-Visible -NIR spectrophotometer was used (Supplementary information).

Fluorescence (FL) analysis was carried out to determine the hindering ability on recombination rate of charge carriers. The excitation dependent FL spectra of CQDs are illustrated in Fig. 2 . When the excitation wavelength is changed from 410 nm to 424 nm, the fluorescence emission peaks exhibit a redshift, indicating the presence of photon reabsorption [23,24]. This observation highlights the excitation-dependent FL characteristic of CQDs. Furthermore, with the rise of the excitation wavelength, intensity changes and redshift in the FL peaks were noticed [25]. The quantum confinement effects and edge defects are associated with the broad FL peak, which changes as the excitation wavelength varies in the brownish fluorescent CQDs [26,27]. On the other hand, FL serves as an efficient approach to gaining insights into the behavior of photogenerated charge carriers (e−/h+) and their recombination possibilities. FL emission commonly results from signals initiated by the recombination of electron-hole pairs. It is estimated that a decrease in emission intensity indicates a lower likelihood of electron-hole pair recombination, thereby contributing to an increase in the photocatalytic efficiency of the catalyst [24].

UV-DRS was used to compute the band-gap energies of the material under investigation (Fig. 3 (a)). The indirect allowed transitions of TiO2, CQDs@TiO2, and CQDs@TNT were estimated using Tauc plots (Eqs. (S1) and (S2), Supplementary information). The experimentally measured band gap energies were 3.02 eV for TiO2, 2.96 eV for CQDs@TiO2, and 2.92 eV for CQDs@TNT. These values align with the visual evidence of a redshift in the absorption edge of CQDs@TNT in comparison to TiO2, as shown in Fig. 3(b). The synthesis process of CQDs may introduce defects in the band structure of TiO2 NTs, causing a slight reduction in the observed band gap. In the case of CQDs@TNT, simultaneous synthesis of CQDs and TiO2 NTs is likely to introduce more defects in the CQDs@TNT, leading to a more significant reduction in the band gap compared to TiO2 and CQDs@TiO2 [28,29].

Further investigation revealed that the CQDs@TNT composite displayed the lowest band gap energy as compared to synthesized samples. The primary cause of narrowing the band gap could be ascribed to the formation of an interfacial electric field between CQDs and TiO2, which alters the Fermi level alignment between these materials [5]. Therefore, when TiO2 and CQDs interact, it enhances the material's ability to absorb visible light. This, in turn, facilitates the generation of more charge carriers, ultimately contributing to an enhancement in photocatalytic activity [30].

To gain insights into the improved separation of charge carriers, it is crucial to investigate the band structure diagram of the TiO2, CQDs@TiO2, and CQDs@TNT heterojunction. The determination of band gaps for TiO2, CQDs@TiO2, and CQDs@TNT was carried out via the Tauc plot in UV-DRS analysis, resulting in band gap values of 3.02 eV, 2.96 eV, and 2.92 eV for TiO2, CQDs@TiO2, and CQDs@TNT, respectively. Correspondingly, the conduction band (CB) edges were found to be −0.94 eV, −1.18 eV, and − 1.54 eV, respectively (Fig. 4 ). These outcomes reveal a notable negative shift in CB of the CQDs@TNT heterojunction in comparison to TiO2 and CQDs@TiO2. The more negatively charged CB potential of CQDs@TNT heterojunction facilitates the movement of excited electrons, leading to the generation of superoxide radicals. Consequently, the altered band structure of the CQDs@TNT composite could enhance the separation of charge carriers, contributing to elevated photocatalytic activity [31–33].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and Raman spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: FTIR was employed to scrutinize the functional groups and chemical composition present in the TiO2, CQDs@TiO2, and CQDs@TNT. The comparison of FTIR spectra for these materials is presented in Fig. 5 (a). The distinctive band found at approximately 1059.8 cm−1 in TiO2 corresponds to Ti-O-Ti bonds. Peaks appearing at 1666.6 cm−1 and within the range of 3250–3500 cm−1 were associated with the expansion of hydroxyl groups in the adsorbed water [34]. Meanwhile, the CQDs exhibited peaks at 1059.8 cm−1 and 2982.2 cm−1, attributed to the out of plane bending vibration of terminal CH bonds and the stretching vibration of asymmetrical CC triple bonds, respectively [35]. In the case of CQDs@TiO2 composites, a broader absorption band emerged below 1449.1 cm−1, signifying the formation of Ti-O-C bonds within the composites. The 2347.1 cm−1 peaks may be associated with the vibrations of CO2 adsorbed on the surface, while the 2353.0 cm−1 band is assigned to gaseous CO2 [36,37]. These finding implies that the surface of CQDs may have relatively high presence of hydroxyl groups in comparison to TiO2, with an increase observed in the hydroxyl groups on CQDs@TiO2 surface, following the formation of complexes between CQDs and TiO2 [36]. Additional peaks at 3737.0 cm−1 and 1666.7 cm−1 in CQDs@TiO2 correspond to OH bending vibrations and CO stretching vibrations, respectively [32]. Furthermore, peaks at 3737.1 cm−1 and 1677.8 cm−1 in TiO2 nanotubes and CQDs@TNT composite are corresponding to the adsorbed water vibrations on its surface. Notably, a new peak located at 1443.3 cm−1 emerged in the CQDs@TNT composite corresponds to CO stretching vibration. The peaks observed at 3451.2 cm−1 in TiO2 NTs and CQDs@TNT composites are assigned to the vibrations of water molecules adsorbed onto the surface [37]. This suggests effective interactions between CQDs and TiO2 nanotubes within the composite material [38].

The structural properties of TiO2, such as phase composition, crystallinity, and crystallite size, can be determined by using the quite sensitive tool of Raman spectroscopy. In Fig. 5(b), the spectra show the presence of four modes centered at 141.0 cm−1 (Eg), 396.0 cm−1 (Eg), 515.0 cm−1 (Bg), and 637.0 cm−1, all attributed to the TiO2 anatase phase [39]. Notably, the decoration of CQDs on TiO2 nanoparticles induces a blue shift in each of these bands. The most prominent mode observed in both TiO2 and CQDs decorated TiO2 NPs corresponds to the low-frequency phonon band of TiO2 anatase phase, centered at 141.5 cm−1 (Eg). The bending vibrations of TiO bonds are corresponding to this mode. Extensive research has examined this low-frequency band to assess the influence of temperature on TiO2 NPs and their dimensions [40]. Furthermore, two more peaks at 515.5 cm−1 and 637.5 cm−1 are observed for CQDs@TiO2 and CQDs@TNT composites in the Raman spectrum, which are attributed to the CQDs D-band and G-band, respectively. This G-band is corresponded to the sp2 carbon atoms in-plane vibration, while the D-band is associated with the occurrence of sp3 carbon atoms. Additionally, in the inset of Fig. 5(b), it is noticeable that the Eg peak experiences a slight blue shift accompanied by peak broadening upon the introduction of CQDs onto TiO2 nanotubes [41]. The outcome indicates the strong interaction between TiO2 and CQDs, and the composite's shifted Raman Eg peak may be caused by the Ti-O-C bonds. These phenomena can be attributed to the surface strain caused by the CQDs decoration on the TiO2 surface atoms, thereby confirming the close interaction between TiO2 and CQDs [42].

The XRD patterns were analyzed for TiO2, CQDs@TiO2, and CQDs@TNT composites in Fig. 6 . This analysis revealed characteristic peaks associated with the TiO2 anatase phase, positioned at 2θ angles of 25.29°, 31.71°, 37.82°, 48.02°, 53.81°, 55.07°, 56.48°, 62.75°, 68.70°, 70.27°, 75.28°, and 84.22°. These angles correspond to the (101), (004), (110), (200), (122), (105), (211), (204), (300), (220), (215), and (224) planes' characteristic peaks, respectively (JCPDS 21–1276) (Fig. 6). In the case of the CQDs@TiO2 sample, similar characteristic peaks of the anatase phase with slight variations in 2θ angles, at 25.29°, 31.87°, 37.98°, 48.16°, 53.81°, 55.22°, 56.64°, 62.75°, 68.86°, 70.43°, 75.28°, and 84.06° were noticed. These angles align with the diffraction patterns of (101), (004), (110), (200), (122), (105), (211), (204), (300), (220), (215), and (224) anatase planes, respectively. Utilizing the Debye-Scherrer equation and considering the reflection angle of 25.29°, the average crystallite size of CQDs@TiO2 was calculated and presented in Table 1 . Furthermore, the analysis focused on the (101) plane of CQDs@TiO2 to determine lattice parameters and cell volume in the tetragonal phase, where a = b ≠ c. Additionally, a minor increase in the unit cell volume was observed, indicating that a small quantity of CQDs may induce crystal defects during the formation of TiO2 crystals. This, in turn, proves advantageous for the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes in the photocatalysis process [43].

In CQDs@TNT sample, a unique pattern of diffraction peaks was observed at 25.29°, 31.71°, 37.98°, 48.10°, 53.82°, 55.22°, 56.64°, 62.75°, 68.70°, 70.43°, 75.44°, and 84.06°, corresponding to the indexed (101), (004), (110), (200), (122), (105), (211), (204), (300), (220), (215), and (224) planes, respectively. The decoration of CQDs onto TiO2 NTs has the effect of reducing particle size of CQDs@TNT and increasing their specific surface area. This alteration results in an enhancement of the catalytic activity for TC degradation. Notably, the results suggest that CQDs@TNT significantly hinder crystalline growth. When TiO2 crystals are being formed, even a low quantity of CQDs can induce crystal defects, which are then helpful in photocatalytic process for separating photogenerated electrons and holes [44,45].
Debye-Scherer's formula was utilized to find the average crystallites size, as the crystallinity is influenced by the size of the crystallite (Eqs. (S3) and (S4), Supplementary information) [46].

Microstrain within the TiO2 lattice was quantified utilizing the uniform deformation model (UDM) of the Williamson-Hall (WH) method, which is demonstrated in Eq. (S5) and Fig. S1(a), (b), (c) of Supplementary information. The UDM approach assumes that the lattice strain is uniformly distributed across all crystallographic directions and treats the crystal's properties as isotropic, meaning they remain consistent regardless of the measurement direction [47]. To compute the strain, the slope of the sample plots between
β hkl
cos θ and 4 sin θ was utilized. Notably, the existence of a compressive strain in all the synthesized samples' anatase phase is indicated by the negative slope determined in the WH plots.

TGA analysis was conducted to examine the effect of temperature on the thermal stability of the synthesized materials. The TGA curves of TiO2, CQDs@TiO2, and CQDs@TNT composites were analyzed under air, nitrogen, and oxygen atmospheres. In air atmosphere (Fig. 7 (a)), each of these TGA curves undergoes the standard thermal decomposition process. Between room temperature and 92.4 °C, the initial region experienced a weight loss of about 4.5 %, which can be attributed to the removal of surplus solvents and physisorbed water molecules from TiO2's surface [48–50]. The adsorption of OH was observed at different temperatures, 93.5 °C and 90.5 °C, with a weight loss of 3.0 % and 12.5 % in nitrogen and oxygen atmospheres, as shown in Fig. 7(b) and (c). In air atmosphere, Fig. 7(d), within the temperature range of 108.0 °C, there was a weight loss of 13.9 %, primarily due to adsorbed moisture [51]. CQDs@TiO2 exhibited a relatively higher weight loss in this regard, conceivably due to an increased presence of surface-adsorbed moisture. The release of surface hydroxylated species was responsible for an additional mass loss of 2.1 % at 225.2 °C [52]. The weight loss in the nitrogen and oxygen atmospheres in CQDs@TiO2 unveiled two significant losses (Fig. 7(e) and (f)). The first, occurring at 261.1 °C, amounted to 73.7 % and was attributed to the removal of physically adsorbed water. The second loss of 8.0 % at 70.0 °C was linked to the presence of volatile components [53]. In CQDs@TNT, the chemisorbed water molecules were observed in air, nitrogen, and oxygen atmospheres at different temperatures: 116.6 °C, 108.0 °C, and 255.3 °C, with corresponding weight losses of 15.0 %, 13.4 %, and 14.5 % (Fig. (g), (h) and (i)) [54]. Finally, in air and nitrogen atmospheres, a weight loss of 3.0 % and 2.1 % at 379.7 °C and 400 °C was attributed to the gradual combustion of carbon within the CQDs. In oxygen atmosphere, the weight loss, 2.5 % at 383.8 °C, can also be ascribed to the desorption of the sample's oxygen, confirming nonstoichiometry (Fig. 7(i)) [55].

XPS analysis was employed to examine the crystal structure and chemical composition, as shown in Fig. 8 . Fig. 8(a) illustrates the deconvolution of the Ti 2p spectrum for TiO2. The binding energies of Ti 2p1/2 and Ti 2p3/2 for TiO2 were detected at 464.2 eV and 458.3 eV, respectively, corresponding to the existence of Ti4+ within the TiO2 lattice (Fig. S2, Supplementary information) [56]. In Fig. 8(b), two peaks were fitted at 464.1 eV and 458.4 eV, corresponding to the Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2 of CQDs@TiO2, respectively. For CQDs@TNT, the deconvolution of Ti 2p occurred at binding energies near 463.8 eV and 458.1 eV, indicative of the spin-orbit coupling of Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, respectively [57]. With a calculated spin-orbit splitting of 5.7 eV between Ti 2p1/2 and Ti 2p3/2, Ti was shown to have a valence state of Ti4+ (tetravalent state) (Fig. 8(c)) [58]. The CQDs decoration on TiO2 nanotubes induced a shift in the XPS spectrum to a reduced state of 456.78 eV. The functional groups on the surface of CQDs transferred partial charge to the Ti4+ centers in TiO2, resulting in a reduction in this binding energy [59].

The analysis of TiO2's O 1 s spectrum deconvolution showed the existence of two peaks at 530.9 eV and 529.8 eV. Non-lattice oxygen is reflected by the peak at 530.9 eV and 529.8 eV (Fig. 8(d)). For the O 1 s spectrum of CQDs@TiO2, the fitted data exhibited two peaks at 531.6 eV and 529.6 eV. The TiO and CO (COO and C-OH) species had binding energies of 531.6 eV and 529.6 eV, respectively (Fig. 8(e)) [28]. The presence of surface hydroxyl groups on the nanocrystalline TiO2's surface can be responsible for the widening around 531.6 eV [29]. In Fig. 8(f), the O 1 s spectrum of CQDs@TNT displayed two distinct peaks at 531.3 eV and 529.7 eV, corresponding to TiO, and CO bonds, respectively [59]. This observation indicates the chemical interaction between TiO2 nanotubes and CQDs occurred in the form of Ti-O-C linkage, in line with FTIR analysis.

To analyze the carbon states within TiO2, the C 1 s spectrum was discretized into two distinct peaks at 288.5 eV and 284.8 eV, which can be assigned to CO and CO bonds (Fig. 8(g)). In the case of CQDs@TiO2, the prominent peak at 284.8 eV is associated with the presence of a CC bonds with sp2 orbitals, and the peak at 286.0 eV is attributed to the CQDs CC bonds (Fig. 8(h)) [28]. For CQDs@TNT, the deconvolution yielded three peaks at 284.7, 285.9, and 288.6 eV, which are assignable to CO, CO, and CC or CC bonds, respectively. Fig. 8(i) shows that CQDs@TNT composites contain carbon‑oxygen bonds [60]. Peaks at 288.6 eV (C–OH), 285.9 eV (hydrocarbons), and 284.7 eV can correspond to adventitious carbon. These results may derive from the impurities introduced during calcination and synthesis processes and from the standards utilized for XPS analysis [61]. It's worth noting that any sample exposed to the atmosphere may have carbon from the surrounding environment present on its surface [62]. Based on the findings from XPS measurements, FTIR, and HR-TEM observations, CQDs@TNT composites are formed as a result of CO bonds that emerge on the surfaces of CQDs and TiO2 nanotubes.

To investigate alterations in the work function and electronic band structure of TiO2, CQDs@TiO2, and CQDs@TNT, UPS was performed Fig. 9 (a)–(c). The valence band maximum (
E VB
) for TiO2 was found to be 2.08 eV above the Fermi level (
E f
). In contrast, for CQDs@TiO2 and CQDs@TNT, this value was measured at 1.78 eV and 1.38 eV below
E f
, respectively, 0.30 eV and 0.70 eV band bending was observed. When forming a CQDs@TNT nanostructure, the difference in work functions causes a shift in the Fermi levels at the semiconductor interface. This shift leads to band bending, impacting the behavior of photo-generated charge transfer [5]. Importantly, the improved separation of photogenerated charge carriers was facilitated by intimate contact at the interfacial junction driven by the built-in electric field emerging from the differing work functions of CQDs and TiO2 [63].

The nanostructure's work function is determined by using Eq. (1).

where 21.21 eV represents the incident ultraviolet photons' energy in He light,
E

is the energy of secondary electron cut-off. The calculated work function values for TiO2, CQDs@TiO2, and CQDs@TNT were found to be 4.70 eV, 4.80 eV, and 4.96 eV, respectively (Fig. 9(c)).

Additionally, the conduction band edges
E CB
was determined using Eq. (2).

The obtained
E CB
were − 0.94 eV for TiO2, −1.18 eV for CQDs@TiO2, and − 1.54 eV for CQDs@TNT. Notably, the CB of the CQDs@TNT semiconductor exhibited a more negative value compared to that of TiO2, while the VB of the CQDs@TNT showed less positive value in comparison to TiO2. These findings suggest that the CB of the CQDs@TNT heterojunction underwent a significant negative shift relative to TiO2, affirming the improved electron injection rate [63]. The photocatalytic activity is further supported by these XPS and UPS investigations, demonstrating the promising potential of CQDs@TNT for diverse applications.

BET analysis was carried out to determine the N2 adsorption-desorption curve and pore size distribution of the synthesized materials (Fig. 10 (a)). The BET surface areas for TiO2, CQDs@TiO2, and CQDs@TNT nanocomposites were determined to be 14.12 m2 g−1, 33.90 m2 g−1, and 39.30 m2 g−1, respectively, with corresponding pore volumes of 0.06 cm3 g−1, 0.16 cm3 g−1, and 0.26 cm3 g−1. Notably, the average pore diameter of the CQDs@TNT sample (26.86 nm) is greater than that of TiO2 (22.14 nm) and CQDs@TiO2 (20.26 nm), as shown in Table 2 . The increased surface area, larger pore volume, and average pore diameter of the CQDs@TNT nanocomposite suggest the presence of more active centers, providing it with a significant advantage in terms of high photocatalytic efficiency [64,65]. This improvement is attributed to the abundant micropores of CQDs distributed on the external surface of the TiO2 NTs. It is noteworthy that the internal pores of TiO2 NTs remain unfilled, with only partial coverage of the external pores [66,67]. Moreover, the existence of small clusters of CQDs particles on the external surface of TiO2 NTs resulted in the creation of mesopores and macropores [68]. The pore size distribution curves, derived using the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method from N2 adsorption isotherms, are illustrated in Fig. 10(b) for the CQDs@TNT sample. The composites of CQDs@TNT exhibited Type IV isotherms, characterized by a nearly horizontal plateau in the central region, indicating an enhanced distribution of pore sizes attributed to the incorporation of CQDs as a modifying material [69].

HR-TEM was used for an exhaustive assessment of the morphology of CQDs, TiO2 NPs, CQDs@TiO2, and CQDs@TNT. The HR-TEM images of the prepared CQDs reveal consistently uniform, nearly spherical or quasi-spherical shapes. The HR-TEM analysis indicates that the CQDs exhibit a uniform size distribution, with dimensions consistently less than 10 nm, as illustrated in Fig. 11 (a). TiO2 NPs, as seen in Fig. 11(b), showcase a spherical shape and around 20.2 nm average particle size. The HR-TEM reveals the occurrence of CQDs on the surface of TiO2 NPs. The average particle size of CQDs@TiO2 measures around 32.0 nm (Fig. 11(c)). In comparison to TiO2, the crystal composite of CQDs@TiO2 has a CQDs covering, giving it a shape closer to a sphere [28]. The CQDs@TNT themselves comprise long cylindrical tubes with a hollow cavity, having an average length of around 100 nm with 20.4 nm of diameter (Fig. 11(d)). Similarly, after annealing at 550 °C, it is observed that the interlayer spacing between the nanotube walls is preserved. These fringes have a spacing of 0.32 nm. It can be concluded that CQDs@TNT is obtained via hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 24 h. Fig. 11(e), (f), (g), and (h) demonstrate that CQDs, TiO2 NPs, CQDs@TiO2, and CQDs@TNT have lattice fringes with average values of 0.24 nm, 0.34 nm, 0.33 nm, and 0.32 nm, respectively. In Fig. 11(i), the SAED images exhibit unique rings, showing the presence of CQDs. These diffraction patterns match with the (101) planes of CQDs. Furthermore, Fig. 11(j) displays SAED patterns that confirm the occurrence of crystalline TiO2, matching the (101) anatase phase of TiO2 [28]. To determine the crystal phases of CQDs and TiO2, SAED patterns were used, which validated that the anatase phase of TiO2 is present and aligned with the XRD results (Fig. 11(k)). The diffraction rings in Fig. 11(k) and (l), clearly distinguishable, confirm the presence of (002) planes, attributable to CQDs, and align with the findings from elemental analysis [50]. According to elemental composition analysis, C, O, and Ti existed in CQDs, TiO2, and CQDs@TiO2 samples (Table S1, S2, and S3, Figs. S3, S4, and S5 of the Supplementary information). In the case of CQDs@TNT, the elemental composition analysis was also studied by the EDS spectra, and the distribution of the elements C, O, and Ti further verifies the successful formation of CQDs@TNT (Fig. S6, Table S4, Supplementary information).

The hydrothermal process employing an alkaline technique is a frequently utilized approach for synthesizing 1D TiO2 nanostructures. In this approach, the TiO2 precursor undergoes treatment with an alkali, typically a solution like NaOH, followed by placement in an autoclave at a designated temperature for a specific duration. The resulting precipitates are subjected to drying and subsequent acid washing [70]. Following this acid treatment, the sample undergoes another drying step and is then subjected to annealing at a specified temperature to yield the required product, as illustrated in Fig. 12 .

During this process, Ti-O-Ti bonds in the titanium precursor break, forming unstable [Ti(OH)6]2 nuclei. These monomers then aggregate into polynuclear complexes through oxolation [71]. Nevertheless, continued growth results in the production of diminutive, thin nanosheets, exhibiting anisotropy in their crystallographic directions, with particularly accelerated growth observed in 1D. The aggregated nanosheets consist of layered unit cells. Both TiO2 and layered titanate are constructed from TiO6 octahedral interconnected through shared edges and corners [72]. The nanosheets exhibit rapid growth in a single direction, ultimately yielding a quasi-1D monoclinic nanostructure, Na2Ti3O7, and between these layers, Na+ ions were positioned in accordance with Eq. (3).
(3)

+ 2NaOH →
Na 2
Ti 3
O 7
+
H 2

(4)
Na 2
TiO 7
+ 2HCl →
H 2
Ti 3
O 7
+ 2NaCl
(5)
H 2
Ti 3
O 7
→
3TiO 2
+
H 2

In the context of these layered titanates, neutral molecules like water find their way into the interstitial spaces, tethered together by the comparatively weak van der-Waals forces of attraction. Due to hydrogen deficiencies at nanosheets' surfaces, considerable surface tension exhibits, leading to bending and curling of these nanosheets. The weak crystallinity in these nanostructures was a result of the Na+ ions that are present between the layers of titanate. Consequently, the acid-washing step, an essential component of alkaline-hydrothermal synthesis, plays a crucial role in enhancing the purity of the resulting nanostructure [70]. The acid-washing process has a significant influence on the synthesized nanostructure's phase and morphology. It has been observed that sodium-rich nanostructures yield mixtures of phases, while protonated titanate nanostructures give rise to the construction of the anatase phase. Eq. (4) demonstrates that when these intermediary structures, specifically the Na2Ti3O7 layered titanate, are subjected to washing with a dilute HCl solution, the H2Ti3O7 is formed by exchanging the Na+ ions with H+ ions [73]. It is imperative to underscore that acid washing boosts the generation of NTs. Consequently, the transition from lamellar sheets to 1D nanostructures primarily develops following the acid washing procedure [71]. Reduced interlayer spacing, due to a decreased Na+/H+ ratio, transformed the morphology into 1D nanostructures. A subsequent heat treatment of the synthesized H2Ti3O7 (dehydration of H2Ti3O7) facilitates the formation of anatase TiO2 (Eq. (5)). Further treatment with HCl converts H2Ti3O7 into metastable TiO2 nanotubes after annealing at elevated temperatures [73].

The photocatalytic activity of these catalysts was meticulously assessed in the degradation of TC within an aqueous solution under visible light, with the findings elucidated in Fig. 13 . The TC concentration for TiO2 was substantially reduced after 120 min of irradiation. However, the initial concentration of TC in CQDs@TiO2 and CQDs@TNT was entirely eliminated after 100 and 80 min, respectively (Fig. 13(a), (b), and (c)) [16]. In the absorption spectrum of a TC aqueous solution, two distinct absorption peaks observed in the 275 to 358 nm range are attributed to the conjugated double-bond structure containing two carbonyl groups and enolic groups, respectively [74]. As the reaction progresses, the distinctive absorption peaks undergo significant reduction. After the degradation of synthesized samples, the characteristic absorption peak diminishes gradually until complete disappearance. Notably, throughout the degradation process, no additional absorption peaks emerge in the UV analysis, indicating the absence of new absorption features during photodegradation [75].

The outcomes underscore the profound impact of CQDs@TNT on enhancing the photocatalytic degradation of TC, in comparison to TiO2 and CQDs@TiO2. Due to the distinctive nanostructure of CQDs@TNT, characterized by a thickness of only a few layers, photogenerated electrons and holes can reach the surface more rapidly compared to TiO2 NPs. This accelerated arrival results in a prolonged lifetime during the recombination process of electrons and holes, indicating efficient separation of photogenerated charge carriers. As a consequence, this typically leads to enhanced photocatalytic activity compared with TiO2 NPs [75,76]. The band gap of CQDs@TNT was noted to decrease compared to TiO2 and CQDs@TiO2, implying that the interaction between TiO2 and CQDs amplifies light absorption in the visible range, consequently boosting the photocatalyst's efficiency under visible light.

The kinetics studies of the degradation of TC revealed that the reaction follows second-order kinetics (Figs. S7, S8(a), (b), and Table S5, Supplementary information). The regression correlation coefficients obtained for both the Langmuir and the Freundlich isotherm are almost similar, but the maximum multilayer capacity determined from the Freundlich isotherm is less than the experimentally determined equilibrium capacity. Therefore, the Freundlich isotherm is not the best model to describe the sorption of TiO2, CQDs@TiO2, and CQDs@TNT. The plot of 1/qe versus 1/Ce is linear, with a higher R2 value, indicating that the sorption process conforms to the Langmuir isotherm, which predicts monolayer coverage. From the slope and the intercept of the Langmuir plot, the values of KL and qm, respectively, are determined (Fig. S9(a), (b), Supplementary information). These values, along with that of R2, are listed in Table S6 in the Supplementary information. Table 3 presents the previous work on TiO2 based photocatalysts and indicates that this work achieved the maximum degradation within 80 min.

Based on the findings from UPS and UV-DRS experiments correlated to scavenger tests and band alignment analysis, a credible mechanism was suggested that explains the accelerated charge separation and enhanced photocatalytic performance of the CQDs@TNT photocatalyst. The smaller band gap of CQDs@TNT in comparison to TiO2 results in enhanced photon absorption, ultimately contributing to increased photocatalytic efficiency. Photogenerated electrons from the CQDs are more likely to recombine with photogenerated holes from TiO2, favouring the photooxidation reaction in TiO2's VB, the photoreduction in the CQDs' CB, and the photooxidation in TiO2's VB [56,80].

The pollutants in TC are degraded into smaller fragments by these active species (Eq. (9)). Either the h+ or OH radicals are responsible for the decomposition of TC molecules (Eqs. (10) and (11)). The results of the scavenger experiment indicate that h+ is primarily accountable for the degradation of TC.

To illustrate the excellent performance provided by our catalyst in the photocatalytic degradation of TC, we compared our results with those from previous studies that utilized different catalysts. Table 3 provides a comparison of the TC photocatalytic degradation performance achieved by our synthesized CQDs@TiO2 photocatalyst with results reported in the literature. This comparison underscores the excellent performance of our catalyst, or photocatalyst, in the context of TC photocatalytic degradation [81,82].

In a photocatalytic reaction, the pH of the solution is a pivotal factor. Existing research suggests that, at high pH values, TiO2 NPs interact favorably with cationic electron donors and electron acceptors for enhancing overall photocatalytic efficiency, whereas at low pH values, they interact favorably with anionic electron donors and electron acceptors. Consequently, TiO2's surface has a negative charge at basic pH conditions, which makes easier electrostatic absorption with the cationic [81]. The photocatalytic efficiency of CQDs decorated TiO2 NTs is influenced by the pH value, as depicted in Fig. 14 . The pH value had a significant impact on the TC's photocatalytic degradation. The efficiency of photocatalytic degradation declined with increasing pH values. The difference can be attributed to the photocatalyst's surface charge characteristics, which subsequently impact the adsorption behavior of TC on the catalyst's surface. In basic medium of CQDs@TNT at pH 10, the cationic nature of TC promotes favorable adsorption, resulting in more photocatalytic activity under visible light compared to TiO2 and CQDs@TiO2 at pH 2 acidic medium.

The potential for reusing the synthesized catalyst was examined in a series of successive photocatalytic investigations. After each cycle of photocatalysis, the catalyst was recovered through centrifugation, washed with distilled water, and then dried at 60 °C for 4 h. Under identical conditions, the retrieved catalyst was combined with a fresh TC solution for successive cycles. This process was repeated four times [5]. The degradation was performed with 10 μM TC, and after each cycle, the centrifuged sample was heated to recover the powder composite for the next cycle. Each subsequent cycle required an additional 5 min in the photocatalytic reaction to degrade TC. The initial concentration of TC in TiO2, CQDs@TiO2, and CQDs@TNT was degraded after 140, 120, and 100 min of irradiation, respectively. The outstanding stability of the CQDs@TNT composite compared to TiO2 and CQDs@TiO2 (Figs. S10 to S12, Supplementary information). Furthermore, the results indicate that the leaching of the CQDs@TNT composite's constituent elements into the water was minimal, highlighting its capability for use in effective wastewater treatment. In summary, these composite photocatalysts exhibit a substantial capacity for enhancing environmentally friendly wastewater treatment processes [5].

In this work, CQDs@TNT were proficiently synthesized using a hydrothermal approach, demonstrating remarkable photocatalytic efficiency in degrading TC, surpassing TiO2 and CQDs@TiO2. The band gap, calculated from UV-DRS, was 3.02 eV for TiO2, 2.96 eV for CQDs@TiO2, and 2.92 eV for CQDs@TNT. The kinetics of the reaction were thoroughly investigated, revealing adherence to a pseudo second-order model. The detailed sorption isotherms study of the synthesized composite concluded that it follows the Langmuir isotherms. The nanocomposite prepared exhibited a considerable improvement in the photocatalytic degradation of TC. This paper will contribute to the advancement of novel catalyst materials for the removal of hazardous and toxic compounds from the environment. The future is encouraging for the development of even more adaptable and simplified fabrication techniques for TiO2 nanocomposite materials.
